title: Review round 1: wolfhsm API idiomaticity changes
notes:
Maintainer risk: MEDIUM — API surface is well-structured and the RAII key-lifetime pattern is sound, but there are 8 specific issues: one logic divergence (mldsa inlines with_key!), one opaque API footgun (NVM access/flags), one real functionality hole (TCP port cap at 32767), two error taxonomy inconsistencies (AuthPermissions no PartialEq, MlDsaKey Ffi vs BadArgs), one undocumented protocol assumption (nvm_list sequential IDs), one library hygiene violation (eprintln! in error path), and one naming trap (wolfhsm_rsa_sign for all RSA ops). None individually is a release blocker, but together they will generate a steady trickle of issue reports and contributor confusion.
Confidence: 15% chance of missed bug. Uncertainty driven by: (1) I cannot see the wolfhsm-sys shims.c or wolfhsm_sys C bindings — if wolfhsm_rsa_sign truly is a general-purpose dispatch shim, that's fine, but if it only wires up sign paths and ignores the op discriminant for decrypt, that's a correctness bug I cannot rule out from the Rust side alone; (2) I cannot verify the wh_Client_NvmList wire protocol — if the server does not guarantee ascending ID ordering, nvm_list.10 is worse than filed (data corruption, not just docs); (3) the wolfhsm-sys generated bindings for whAuthPermissions are not visible — if that struct contains padding bytes, derived PartialEq on it would be wrong and wmp.2 is a correctness bug, not just ergonomics. Would raise confidence if: I could read shims.c to confirm wolfhsm_rsa_sign is truly a full RSA primitive dispatcher; and if I could read the wolfHSM C NvmList docs to confirm monotone-ascending ID semantics. Running out of real complaints: yes. The two issues I labeled opinion (wmp.17, wmp.9) are the only ones I found where the engineering case does not survive scrutiny. Every other filed issue has a real engineering basis.
Review converged after 1 round(s). P0=2 P1=8 P2=1 found, all fixed. Final review: wolfssl-rs-wmp.
